Title: IRBuilder.if_else() and IRBuilder.if_then() don't seem to work - llvmlite
URL: https://numba.discourse.group/t/irbuilder-if-else-and-irbuilder-if-then-dont-seem-to-work/2267
Content: # IRBuilder.if\_else() and IRBuilder.if\_then() don't seem to work. Not sure if this belongs to `llvmlite` channel or the main `numba` channel, but it seems like `IRBuilder.if_then` and `IRBuilder.if_else` aren’t working. I am trying to write a very simple `numba` `intrinsic` function that will return `1` if the supplied value `x` is greater than `0` or return `1` otherwise. The `intrinsic` function looks like below code snippet, it’s called `check_value()`:. def codegen(context, builder, signature, args):. gtz = builder.icmp_signed(">", args[0], args[0].type(0)). a = context.get_constant(signature.args[0], 1). and the function to be `njit`ted looks like this:. and this is how it’s been used:. and when I run it, I am getting this output:. How do I make it work? As the name implies, `codegen` is a function that generates code, the LLVM intermediate representation (IR). Since the otherwise case is executed last (at compile time), `a` will always be a constant with the value `0`, which you have already observed. gtz = builder.icmp_signed(">", args[0], llty(0)).

Title: Building and using llvmlite - a basic example - Eli Bendersky's website
URL: https://eli.thegreenplace.net/2015/building-and-using-llvmlite-a-basic-example
Content: llvmlite was created last year by the developers of Numba (a JIT compiler for scientific Python), and just recently replaced llvmpy as their bridge to LLVM. One of the reasons the Numba devs decided to ditch llvmpy in favor of a new approach is the biggest issue heavy users of LLVM as a library have - its incredible rate of API-breaking change. The LLVM C++ API is notoriously unstable and will remain this way for the foreseeable future. Since building the IR using repeated FFI calls to LLVM proved to be slow and error-prone in llvmpy, llvmlite re-implemented the IR builder in pure Python. This also reduces the "API surface" llvmlite uses, since the textual representation of LLVM IR is one of the more stable things in LLVM. LLVM has a great tool named llvm-config in its binary image, and the Makefile in llvmlite uses it, which means building llvmlite with any version of LLVM I want is just a simple matter of running:.

Title: Writing Fibonacci in LLVM with llvmlite - Ian Bertolacci
URL: https://ian-bertolacci.github.io/posts/writing_fibonacci_in_LLVM_with_llvmlite
Content: from __future__ import print_function from llvmlite import ir importllvmlite.binding as llvm from ctypes import CFUNCTYPE, c_int64""" Generate fibonacci function: f(n) = 1 if n <= 1 else f(n-1) + f(n-2) """ # Create a 64bit wide int type int_type = ir. Module(name = "m_fibonacci_example") # Create the Fibonacci function and block fn_fib = ir. Function(module, fn_int_to_int_type, name = "fn_fib") fn_fib_block = fn_fib. append_basic_block(name = "fn_fib_entry") # Create the builder for the fibonacci code block builder = ir. Constant(int_type, 2); # Create inequality comparison instruction fn_fib_n_lteq_1 = builder. ret(fn_fib_rec_res) # Print the generated LLVM asm code print(module)""" Execute generated code. get_function_address("fn_fib") # Run the function via ctypes c_fn_fib = CFUNCTYPE(c_int64, c_int64)(func_ptr)# Test our function for n in 0..50 for n in xrange(0, 50 + 1): result = c_fn_fib(n) print("c_fn_fib({0}) = {1}". # Create the Fibonacci function and block fn_fib = ir. Function(module, fn_int_to_int_type, name = "fn_fib") fn_fib_block = fn_fib. # Create inequality comparison instruction fn_fib_n_lteq_1 = builder.

Title: IR builders — llvmlite 0.42.0 documentation
URL: https://llvmlite.pydata.org/en/v0.42.0/user-guide/ir/ir-builder.html
Content: It allows you to fill the basic blocks of your functions with LLVM instructions. An `IRBuilder` internally maintains a current basic block and a pointer inside the block’s list of instructions. When a new instruction is added, it is inserted at that point, and then the pointer is advanced after the new instruction. A `IRBuilder` also maintains a reference to metadata describing the current source location, which is attached to all inserted instructions. If *block*—a `Block`—is given, the builder starts at the end of this basic block. :   The basic block that the builder is operating on. :   The function that the builder is operating on. :   Append a basic block, with the given optional *name*, to the current function. The current block is also changed to the instruction’s basic block. :   Position at the end of the basic *block*. :   Position the builder either at the end of the basic *block*, if it is not terminated, or just before the *block*’s terminator:.
